agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only — a separate space that accepts the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. `documentBlocks` is the default: put anything a human reader needs there. Use `agentDocumentBlocks` only for content that helps an agent build correctly but is noise for humans. For example: hard MUST/MUST-NOT rules an agent should treat as firm limits; notes that correct a mistake an agent is likely to make, such as reaching for this entity when a confusable one fits better; constraints backed by test-run evidence; and machine-checkable criteria with verification modes. A hard rule that humans also need (such as an accessibility requirement) belongs in `documentBlocks`, not here. Agent blocks add to the human docs — they never replace or duplicate them. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human blocks first.

guide.description: A long-form, reading-oriented document: a getting-started walkthrough, a contribution guide, a tutorial, a conceptual overview, a migration guide, or any narrative guidance the system needs. Guides fill the gap left by the artifact-bound entities (component, token, foundation, pattern). Those entities document a single thing, not a journey or a concept. The top level holds its identity: `identifier`, `name`, and `description`. The `metadata` object holds optional fields. Use `category` (e.g., 'getting-started', 'contribution', 'tutorial', 'concept', 'migration') to classify the guide, and `tags` for finer-grained discovery. The `documentBlocks` array holds structured docs as typed entries: sections (documentation content), steps (procedures), guidelines, use-cases, imports, accessibility, and content.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a guide.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the guide (e.g., 'getting-started', 'contributing', 'theming-tutorial', 'migrating-to-v2'). MUST be lowercase kebab-case and unique within its entity group.

name.description: Display name shown in docs (e.g., 'Getting started', 'Contributing', 'Theming tutorial', 'Migrating to v2').

description.description: What this guide covers, who it is for, and what the reader will accomplish. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json). Use `category` to classify the guide (e.g., 'getting-started', 'contribution', 'tutorial', 'concept', 'migration').

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this guide to the entities it documents or depends on. Inverse edges are derived by tooling. Use the `links` metadata field for external resources.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this guide, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. It accepts the guide-specific kind (steps), the reused imports kind (setup instructions), plus the general kinds (guidelines, use-cases, accessibility, content, sections, checklist). The free-form sections block is a guide's backbone. The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. A guide reads top to bottom, so order matters and tools SHOULD keep it for display. Put links to external resources in the `links` metadata field or a section's `links`, not in document block entries; point at other entities through the `relationships` array.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only — a separate space that accepts the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. `documentBlocks` is the default: put anything a human reader needs there. Use `agentDocumentBlocks` only for content that helps an agent build correctly but is noise for humans. For example: hard MUST/MUST-NOT rules an agent should treat as firm limits; notes that correct a mistake an agent is likely to make, such as reaching for this entity when a confusable one fits better; constraints backed by test-run evidence; and machine-checkable criteria with verification modes. A hard rule that humans also need (such as an accessibility requirement) belongs in `documentBlocks`, not here. Agent blocks add to the human docs — they never replace or duplicate them. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human blocks first.

pattern.description: A repeated, multi-component solution to a common UX problem. Patterns sit above individual components. They show how those components combine to meet a user need. The top level holds its identity: `identifier`, `name`, and `description`. The `metadata` object holds the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json). The `documentBlocks` array holds structured docs as typed entries: `interactions` for flow steps, `guidelines` for rules, `accessibility` for a11y specs, and so on.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a pattern.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the pattern (e.g., 'error-messaging', 'navigation', 'empty-state', 'form-validation'). MUST be unique within its entity group.

name.description: Display name shown in docs (e.g., 'Error Messaging', 'Navigation', 'Empty State').

description.description: What this pattern is, the UX problem it solves, and how its components compose to meet a user need. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this pattern to other entities — chiefly 'composes' the components it is built from (mark `required` true for essential ones), plus 'depends-on' and 'alternative-to'. Use the `links` metadata field for external resources. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this pattern, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. Patterns accept the pattern-specific kind (interactions), the shared structural kinds (anatomy, variants, states), plus the general kinds (guidelines, use-cases, accessibility, content, sections, checklist). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. Order matters, so tools SHOULD keep it for display. Interaction entries rely on order to show the flow over time. Declare the components a pattern uses as typed `composes` edges in the entity's `relationships` array (with `role` describing what each component does), not in a dedicated block kind.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only — a separate space that accepts the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. `documentBlocks` is the default: put anything a human reader needs there. Use `agentDocumentBlocks` only for content that helps an agent build correctly but is noise for humans. For example: hard MUST/MUST-NOT rules an agent should treat as firm limits; notes that correct a mistake an agent is likely to make, such as reaching for this entity when a confusable one fits better; constraints backed by test-run evidence; and machine-checkable criteria with verification modes. A hard rule that humans also need (such as an accessibility requirement) belongs in `documentBlocks`, not here. Agent blocks add to the human docs — they never replace or duplicate them. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human blocks first.

tokenOverride.description: A single token override inside a theme: which token changes, and why. The override value lives in the DTCG file. This entry records the intent and reason.

token.description: The identifier of the token being overridden. MUST match the `identifier` of a token defined elsewhere in the system. Token identifiers follow no fixed pattern, so they can support DTCG and design tool naming styles (e.g., 'color-text-primary', 'color.text.primary', 'color/text/primary').

description.description: Says what changes for this token in this theme and why (e.g., 'Lightened to meet WCAG AAA contrast on the dark background'). The resolved value lives in the DTCG file.

theme.description: A named context that adapts the design system's token values. DSDS captures the purpose, usage document blocks, accessibility notes, and the list of affected tokens. The override values live in the DTCG file that `source` points to. Use themes for color modes (light, dark, high-contrast), density settings (compact, comfortable), brand variants, or product-specific tweaks. The token catalog stays the source of truth for token identity, type, and docs. The `metadata` object holds the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a theme.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the theme (e.g., 'dark', 'high-contrast', 'compact', 'brand-sub-brand').

name.description: Display name for the theme (e.g., 'Dark Mode', 'High Contrast', 'Compact Density').

description.description: What this theme is for, the context it adapts the system to, and when to apply it. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

source.description: Points to the W3C Design Tokens (DTCG) definition. DSDS does not copy token values. The DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type info.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this theme's token override definitions.

path.description: The dot-path to this theme's group within the DTCG file, if applicable.

overrides.description: Token overrides this theme applies. Each entry says which token changes and why. List only tokens whose values differ from the default. Unlisted tokens keep their default values. The override values live in the DTCG file that `source` points to.

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this theme to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' the tokens it overrides, 'extends' a base theme. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this theme, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. Themes accept the general kinds only (guidelines, use-cases, accessibility, content, sections, checklist). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. Order matters, so tools SHOULD keep it for display.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only — a separate space that accepts the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. `documentBlocks` is the default: put anything a human reader needs there. Use `agentDocumentBlocks` only for content that helps an agent build correctly but is noise for humans. For example: hard MUST/MUST-NOT rules an agent should treat as firm limits; notes that correct a mistake an agent is likely to make, such as reaching for this entity when a confusable one fits better; constraints backed by test-run evidence; and machine-checkable criteria with verification modes. A hard rule that humans also need (such as an accessibility requirement) belongs in `documentBlocks`, not here. Agent blocks add to the human docs — they never replace or duplicate them. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human blocks first.

token.description: Docs for a single design token: identifier, type, use cases, guidelines, and accessibility notes. The display `name` property is optional. When it is omitted, the `identifier` serves as the display label. Description and status are optional. The `metadata` object holds the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json). When a token sits inside a token group and omits its own status field, the parent group's status applies.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a token.

identifier.description: The token identifier. It follows no fixed pattern, so it can support DTCG and design tool naming styles that use dots, slashes, or other separators (e.g., 'color-text-primary', 'color.text.primary', 'color/text/primary'). It also serves as the human-readable display label.

name.description: Optional display name (e.g., 'Primary Text'). When omitted, the `identifier` serves as the display label — the uniform rule for all entities is: display `name` when present, otherwise `identifier`.

tokenType.description: The type of token aligned with the DTCG spec (e.g., 'color', 'dimension', 'fontFamily', 'fontWeight', 'duration', 'cubicBezier', 'number', 'shadow'). MUST be present unless an ancestor token group declares `tokenType` — children inherit the group's value when they omit their own. Validators enforce the own-or-inherited rule; a token with no `tokenType` anywhere in its ancestry is a defect.

description.description: What this token represents and when to use it. Optional, so keep it terse at scale. CommonMark supported.

source.description: Points to the W3C Design Tokens (DTCG) definition. DSDS does not copy token values. The DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type info.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this token's definition.

path.description: The dot-path to this token within the DTCG file (e.g., 'color.text.primary').

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' a token this one aliases, 'part-of' a token group. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this token, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. Tokens accept the general kinds only (guidelines, use-cases, accessibility, content, sections, checklist). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. Order matters, so tools SHOULD keep it for display.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only — a separate space that accepts the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. `documentBlocks` is the default: put anything a human reader needs there. Use `agentDocumentBlocks` only for content that helps an agent build correctly but is noise for humans. For example: hard MUST/MUST-NOT rules an agent should treat as firm limits; notes that correct a mistake an agent is likely to make, such as reaching for this entity when a confusable one fits better; constraints backed by test-run evidence; and machine-checkable criteria with verification modes. A hard rule that humans also need (such as an accessibility requirement) belongs in `documentBlocks`, not here. Agent blocks add to the human docs — they never replace or duplicate them. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human blocks first.

tokenGroup.description: A group of related design tokens. Token groups are flexible units: a full collection, a family (e.g., all color tokens), a sub-family (e.g., a single hue with its grade scale), or any other logical grouping. Groups can nest to form a deep hierarchy. A top-level group might stand for 'color' and hold child groups for 'color-text', 'color-background', and 'color-border', each with its own tokens. This nesting mirrors how real design systems organize tokens into families and sub-families. The display `name` property is optional. When it is omitted, the `identifier` serves as the display label. Description and status are optional. The `metadata` object holds the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json). When a child omits its own status field, the parent group's status applies.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a token-group.

identifier.description: The token group identifier. It follows no fixed pattern, so it can support DTCG and design tool naming styles (e.g., 'color-text', 'color.text', 'color/text'). It also serves as the human-readable display label.

name.description: Optional display name (e.g., 'Primary Text'). When omitted, the `identifier` serves as the display label — the uniform rule for all entities is: display `name` when present, otherwise `identifier`.

description.description: What this token group covers and how it is organized. Optional, so keep it terse at scale. CommonMark supported.

tokenType.description: When all tokens in this group share the same DTCG token type, declare it here so you don't repeat it on each child. Each token in children MAY override this value. Common values: 'color', 'dimension', 'fontFamily', 'fontWeight', 'duration', 'cubicBezier', 'number', 'shadow'.

source.description: Points to the W3C Design Tokens (DTCG) definition. DSDS does not copy token values. The DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type info.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this group's definition.

path.description: The dot-path to this group within the DTCG file (e.g., 'color.text').

children.description: An ordered array of tokens and/or nested token groups inside this group. Each item MUST be either a token or a tokenGroup. Order often shows a meaningful progression (e.g., lightest to darkest, or smallest to largest), so it matters and tools SHOULD keep it for display. Children inherit tokenType and status from this group when they omit their own values.

metadata.description: Optional metadata: the shared entityMetadata fields (see metadata/metadata.schema.json).

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' a token this one aliases, 'part-of' a token group. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this token group, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. It accepts the general kinds only (guidelines, use-cases, accessibility, content, sections, checklist). Use this array for docs that apply to the group as a whole. Put docs for individual tokens on the token inside `children`.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only — a separate space that accepts the same block kinds as `documentBlocks`. `documentBlocks` is the default: put anything a human reader needs there. Use `agentDocumentBlocks` only for content that helps an agent build correctly but is noise for humans. For example: hard MUST/MUST-NOT rules an agent should treat as firm limits; notes that correct a mistake an agent is likely to make, such as reaching for this entity when a confusable one fits better; constraints backed by test-run evidence; and machine-checkable criteria with verification modes. A hard rule that humans also need (such as an accessibility requirement) belongs in `documentBlocks`, not here. Agent blocks add to the human docs — they never replace or duplicate them. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays, human blocks first.

anyEntity.description: The required entity `kind` property identifies its type and selects its schema via one `if`/`then` branch per kind. This checks that an entity is referencing the correct schema.

fileRef.description: A JSON Reference to a DSDS entity or entity group defined in another file. Tooling ( json-schema-ref-parser) resolves the reference before validation and bundling. The URI should include a JSON Pointer fragment to target a specific value within the referenced file (ex: './button.dsds.json#/entity', './groups/tokens.dsds.json#/entityGroups/0').

$ref.description: A relative reference to a DSDS file, optionally with a JSON Pointer fragment that identifies the target value within that file (ex: './button.dsds.json#/entity'). The value MUST be relative. The pattern rejects absolute paths, protocol-relative references, and scheme-bearing URIs (http:, file:, …). A reference to another system uses `extends.url`. Resolvers MUST detect cycles. They MUST treat unresolvable files or pointers and shape mismatches as fatal. They MUST gate any remote dereferencing behind an allow-list (see the spec's reference-resolution and security rules).

entityGroup.description: A named collection of design system entities. All entities live in a single `entities` array, in display order, mixing entity types freely. Each entity's required `kind` identifies its type, so no per-type arrays are needed. A group MUST hold at least one entity.

name.description: Human-readable name of the collection (ex: 'Acme Design System', 'Color Tokens', 'Button Documentation'). This acts as display label only.

entities.description: The group's entities, in order of default display. A group can contain any mix of entity kinds. Items may be inlined or referenced through a $ref object that points to another DSDS file. Order is significant: tools SHOULD keep it for display.
